tweets.text:
Look: 95% of post‑Hyperliquid perp DEXs are failing to acquire users.

I read this as a liquidity‑moat + switching‑cost problem amplified by a broken airdrop meta.

Hyperliquid still commands ~62% of perp DEX open interest, so traders pay real slippage/operational costs to move.

At the same time 92.9% of tokens launched 2024–2026 trade below TGE. No airdrop farming now – it's practically dead.

So I have a question:
Why do founders keep launching new perp DEXs every week?

tweets.text:
Robinhood has generated $1.8M from ~110M transactions since July 1.

Here's the rough revenue split:

→ @RobinhoodApp: 89%
→ @arbitrum: 10%
→ @ethereum: 0.15%

Meanwhile, Ethereum has only earned ~$11K in settlement fees. Just look at the activity on the chain:

→ $608K revenue in the last 24 hours
→ 256% weekly growth
→ $396M in stablecoin supply
→ 1.5M active addresses

So is there a future for the Robinhood chain?

The funny part is that Robinhood Chain was pitched around tokenized stocks, but they currently account for only ~$13M, compared with nearly $400M in stablecoins and billions in DEX volume.

None of that means it's dead. Memes bootstrapped Base and Solana too. But it does mean the current numbers are tied to hype.

IMO, since they previously had ~28M users, they have a unique distribution edge.

Once the initial hype fades, we’ll have a clearer read on demand. IMO, Robinhood is well positioned in this market.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Robinhood is pumping rn, and it’s cool to see all these protocols bringing traditional finance onchain ↓

• @arcus_xyz | DEX for trading tokenized stocks and crypto, with perpetual futures planned.

• @rialto_xyz | Onchain spot exchange using market-maker quotes to trade https://t.co/CVqHcrG4m9

So is there a future for the Robinhood chain?

The funny part is that Robinhood Chain was pitched around tokenized stocks, but they currently account for only ~$13M, compared with nearly $400M in stablecoins and billions in DEX volume.

None of that means it's dead. Memes bootstrapped Base and Solana too. But it does mean the current numbers are tied to hype.

IMO, since they previously had ~28M users, they have a unique distribution edge.

Once the initial hype fades, we’ll have a clearer read on demand. IMO, Robinhood is well positioned in this market.

tweets.text:
My wind down list (unfortunately) continues.

More protocols that announced shutdowns this year ↓

• @zerodotnetwork | Zerion’s gasless Ethereum L2. Users must withdraw their assets before the network stops producing blocks on July 31.

• @Powerloom | Decentralized data network. Hosted services began closing in June, with its chain scheduled to shut down permanently on July 21.

• @SatoriFinance | Perpetual-futures DEX. Operations ended on July 16 after its revenue became insufficient to sustain the platform.

• @ionicmoney | Multichain lending protocol. Ceased operations following the damage caused by its 2025 exploit.

• @EverclearOrg | Cross-chain clearing protocol formerly known as Connext. The protocol was sunset, while its Foundation and Labs began winding down.

• @zerolendxyz | Multichain lending protocol. Began winding down because inactive chains, shrinking liquidity, thin margins and growing security risks made it unsustainable.

• @PolynomialFi | Onchain derivatives protocol. Closed Polynomial Trade and Polynomial Chain, with the final chain shutdown taking place on March 3.

• @milky_way_zone | Liquid-staking protocol. Announced its permanent wind-down in January.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We've made the very difficult decision of winding down Zapper.

Thank you for being part of this crazy journey with us ⚡️🫶

You can learn more about the sunset here.
tweets.full_text:
My wind down list (unfortunately) continues.

More protocols that announced shutdowns this year ↓

• @zerodotnetwork | Zerion’s gasless Ethereum L2. Users must withdraw their assets before the network stops producing blocks on July 31.

• @Powerloom | Decentralized data network. Hosted services began closing in June, with its chain scheduled to shut down permanently on July 21.

• @SatoriFinance | Perpetual-futures DEX. Operations ended on July 16 after its revenue became insufficient to sustain the platform.

• @ionicmoney | Multichain lending protocol. Ceased operations following the damage caused by its 2025 exploit.

• @EverclearOrg | Cross-chain clearing protocol formerly known as Connext. The protocol was sunset, while its Foundation and Labs began winding down.

• @zerolendxyz | Multichain lending protocol. Began winding down because inactive chains, shrinking liquidity, thin margins and growing security risks made it unsustainable.

• @PolynomialFi | Onchain derivatives protocol. Closed Polynomial Trade and Polynomial Chain, with the final chain shutdown taking place on March 3.

• @milky_way_zone | Liquid-staking protocol. Announced its permanent wind-down in January.

tweets.text: Founder mode means running a company the way a founder can and a hired CEO can't. We don't yet know all the things only a founder can do. But it's not an imprecise term. It's like the definition of a Mersenne prime. We know exactly what one is, but we only know 52 so far.

tweets.text:
For me, Solana has always been the ecosystem that brings together almost all the best projects people actually want to use.

Starting tomorrow, you can drop the „almost“.

Beezie expanding to Solana is a massive win for everyone. It brings one of the best onchain collectible platforms in the industry to the chain with the deepest collector culture in crypto, unlocks a whole new user base for Beezie, and gives Solana collectors a product they’ll genuinely love.

I usually don’t care much about ecosystem expansions, but I do when it makes sense and everyone comes out ahead.

@Solana winning. @Beezie winning. Collectors winning.

tweets.full_text: While TSLA shares will likely be down tomorrow, the long term story continues to improve. Elon is all in on capex, FSD adoption is accelerating, Robotaxi is rolling out slowly to prioritize safety before scaling, and the odds of a SpaceX-Tesla merger just inched higher. https://t.co/9SQva4mVSY

tweets.text:
More on capex from the $TSLA call.

Capex is for scaling factors: TeraFab, Solar manufacturing, Optimus, Cybercab, Semi.

Elon says it’s a capital efficiency vs. time tradeoff question. It’s ok to be less capital efficient in the near term if they get stuff done faster because that will deliver a higher NPV later. 

My take:  Elon's all in on capex, as long as it’s not “wasteful.” I expect 2027 to be $25B vs. Street at $21B
tweets.full_text:

Capex is for scaling factors: TeraFab, Solar manufacturing, Optimus, Cybercab, Semi.

Elon says it’s a capital efficiency vs. time tradeoff question. It’s ok to be less capital efficient in the near term if they get stuff done faster because that will deliver a higher NPV later. 

My take:  Elon's all in on capex, as long as it’s not “wasteful.” I expect 2027 to be $25B vs. Street at $21B

tweets.text:
The odds that $TSLA and $SPCX come together just increased.

They got a question about the topic on the call, and I was surprised they even entertained it. Elon said they have a great relationship but couldn't comment on further integration, handing it off to legal.

After legal said they couldn't say much, Elon took the mic back and elaborated on the benefits the two companies bring each other, like Starlink preventing Robotaxis from going into a "Bermuda Triangle of connectivity." He said there are other benefits, too.

My take: Going into the call, I thought there was an 80% chance the two companies come together in the next few years. I'm raising that to 90%.
tweets.full_text:
The odds that $TSLA and $SPCX come together just increased.

They got a question about the topic on the call, and I was surprised they even entertained it. Elon said they have a great relationship but couldn't comment on further integration, handing it off to legal.

After legal said they couldn't say much, Elon took the mic back and elaborated on the benefits the two companies bring each other, like Starlink preventing Robotaxis from going into a "Bermuda Triangle of connectivity." He said there are other benefits, too.

My take: Going into the call, I thought there was an 80% chance the two companies come together in the next few years. I'm raising that to 90%.

tweets.text:
Question from the call on Robotaxi rollout: What milestones are you looking for to increase the pace of rollout?

Elon said the economics will be "so compelling" and "demand will outstrip supply". 

Elon says the hold up on getting more aggressive at the rollout is the march of nines. That is does 99.9% safe enough or 99.99999%.

He is saying he wants more data on the safety. They have 380k miles. It's not enough to have confidence in a full rollout.  $TSLA

tweets.text:
$TSLA says they now have 380k miles of unsupervised Robotaxi with zero incidents. 

My quick math is that its about 20% of total Robotaxi miles and about 1% of compared to Waymo.

I'm fine with that gap between Waymo given Tesla is making the right call to putting safety first.

tweets.text:
Welcome deliveries back to the $TSLA story.

Elon talked about growing the core business. The CFO says they have the strongest backlog since 2023 and won't be able to keep up with demand.

Street is looking for deliveries to be down 8% yy in September. I expect them to be flat. For December Street at up 10%, based on CFO comment its likely 10-15%. 

Deliveries matter because more cars mean more shots on goal for FSD and learnings for autonomy.

tweets.text:
$TSLA dips another 2% on Elon's comment that this will be a massive capex year. He didn't detailed numbers. We likely get them from CFO later on the call.

The key is next year. Street at $25B in capex for CY26 and $21B in CY27. That number next year is likely above $25B.

tweets.text:
$GOOG dips slightly more. Now down 4% on CFOs comment that Cloud margins they will go down in the back half of this year because they need to go to third parties, aka $SPCX , to meet the demand. $GOOG

As for TPU's would not answer the question about margins on TPU vs Cloud, now at 35.6% which is a record. TPUs are likely dilutive to margins but it's still small.

tweets.text:
$GOOG CFO says TPU sales are recognized in the $514B in Cloud backlog. The TPU number is small and will become more measurable in 2027.

Bottom line: TPU does not move the needle for GOOG and is not competitive enough to measurable hurt $NVDA.

$GOOG stock drops about 3% in after hours on the capex guide, about 4% higher than the Street for this year and they said it would continue to aggressively invest next year. 

Translation: Street looking for about 37% growth next year. I expect analyst increase that to 45% tomorrow, and eventually it will be above 55%.

tweets.text:
New $GOOG AI Mode ads are “gaining traction”. 

Examples are text ads during trip planning, and putting clearly marked ads inside of AI Mode responses. 

This is important because it’s a lever that the company can continue to leverage on to keep Search growth rates in the mid teens (great at 17% in June). They did the same thing in 2010-2015 as the company slowing turned up mobile search ad coverage.

tweets.text:
$TSLA down 3% because of the miss on auto margins ex credits. It came in at 16.3% vs. Street at 18.5% and 19.2% in March. I expected it to be 19%. 

Basically says there was discounting or a spike in cost of goods. Both are directionally negative. 

We will get more on the call.
tweets.full_text:
$TSLA down 3% because of the miss on auto margins ex credits. It came in at 16.3% vs. Street at 18.5% and 19.2% in March. I expected it to be 19%. 

Basically says there was discounting or a spike in cost of goods. Both are directionally negative. 

We will get more on the call.

tweets.text:
TSLA most important number is FSD subs grew 56% vs. 51% March. That growth should accelerate in September because of the huge delivery beat in June. 

In the investor deck they report “with over 55% of new deliveries including FSD subscriptions”. I assume they are talking about paid subs, not the free one month trail that comes with new Tesla's. 

If that’s the case that attach rates are 55% in the US, that is higher my estimate of sub 10% attach rates late in 2024. This underscores how much FSD has improved. I believe about 20% of global Tesla’s with Hardware 3 now subscribe to FSD. That number likely goes to 85% long term.
tweets.full_text:
TSLA most important number is FSD subs grew 56% vs. 51% March. That growth should accelerate in September because of the huge delivery beat in June. 

In the investor deck they report “with over 55% of new deliveries including FSD subscriptions”. I assume they are talking about paid subs, not the free one month trail that comes with new Tesla's. 

If that’s the case that attach rates are 55% in the US, that is higher my estimate of sub 10% attach rates late in 2024. This underscores how much FSD has improved. I believe about 20% of global Tesla’s with Hardware 3 now subscribe to FSD. That number likely goes to 85% long term.

tweets.text:
$GOOG Cloud up 82% y/y vs Street at up 63% and the stock is flat because fractional misses Search and Margins

Search: Street was looking for 17.1% growth, came in at 16.8%. 

Operating Margins: Came in at 34.0% vs. Street at 34.5%.

My take: Given what we now know, the stock should be up tomorrow. The Cloud number is the most important number and it was a massive beat. Now we have to wait for the call.

tweets.text:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗟𝗢𝗪𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗧𝗨𝗥𝗡𝗢𝗩𝗘𝗥 𝗔𝗖𝗖𝗢𝗨𝗡𝗧 𝗪𝗔𝗦 𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗕𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗣𝗘𝗥𝗙𝗢𝗥𝗠𝗘𝗥: 𝟵𝟳% 𝗬𝗧𝗗

I haven’t provided a half-year report this year, so I thought now would be a good time.

All client accounts are currently up between approximately 60% and 97% this year, depending on the strategy.

The account that performed best was not the one where I traded the most.

It was a highly concentrated stock portfolio that I mostly left untouched and allowed to run. The only regular trading was in the $QQQ ratio spreads, which I generally placed once every two weeks.

Here is where the profits came from:

Approximately 20% came from the major oil call I made and shared here at the start of the year. You can see the tweet pinned to my profile.

Around 30% came from $QQQ ratio spreads. I explained here in detail how I structured them, when I placed them and under which market scenarios they were designed to perform.

The remaining 40% came from individual stock ideas I discussed here.

10% from $VIX, short $UVXY and recent oil spike, on which I tweeted as well beforehand.

Nothing fancy. No semiconductors. Not a single one.

Just concentration, patience, $QQQ options structuring and very little trading.

tweets.text:
When software was expensive - thin, horizontal, best-of-breed software stacks extracted rents across every business. 

Now that software is cheap - value moves to vertically integrated businesses that deliver opinionated end-to-end experiences.

Now that software is cheap - value moves to vertically integrated businesses that deliver opinionated end-to-end experiences.

No victory laps yet.  I have admiration for the Senators, staffers, industry team and lobbyists who have grinded for over a year on this!   

I will buy drinks for all when this gets signed into law!

tweets.text: Using principles is a way of both simplifying and improving your decision making. While it might seem obvious to you by now, it’s worth repeating that realizing that almost all “cases at hand” are just “another one of those,” identifying which “one of those” it is, and then applying well-thought-out principles for dealing with it. #principleoftheday

tweets.text:
Someone who will have the balls to ask the embarrassing questions:

- Why did you buy $2bn worth of BTC at $81k with all your cash to then sell MSTR 30% below for cash? Why aren't you more conservative in anticipation instead of being a panic seller after 30% dumps (for the 2nd time after the creation of the USD reserve) ?
- Why do you keep selling MSTR instead of BTC below the 1.22x mNAV threshold level that you emphasized by yourself?
- Why don't you say that the ATM at $100 isn't guaranteed anymore? Why do you keep targeting a 1% STRC range that is mathematically impossible to reach?

tweets.text:
Sad and ironic that 

1. HYPE is the largest espouser of DeFi, whose whole raison d'etre is transparency as one of its core ideals (it's a decentralized perp dex)

2. The largest HF in the space has lost the plot on crypto, and now wants privacy

Overton window has shifted...
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Yesterday we unstaked a large slug of HYPE. We did not unstake to sell.

This is why privacy matters. Our funds are constantly tracked, forcing regular wallet rotations. Institutions need privacy to operate. This is why we’re long $ZAMA and $ZEC. Zama unlocks private defi, Zcash
